elements.sent-2411-001: In continuous time, the laws of martingales tend to be singular to each other.
elements.sent-2411-002: Notably, N.\ Gantert introduced the concept of specific relative entropy between real-valued continuous martingales, defined as a scaling limit of finite-dimensional relative entropies, and showed that this quantity is non-trivial despite the aforementioned mutual singularity of martingale laws.
elements.sent-2411-003: Our main mathematical contribution is to extend this object, originally restricted to one-dimensional martingales, to multiple dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-004: Among other results, we establish that Gantert's inequality, bounding the specific relative entropy with respect to Wiener measure from below by an explicit functional of the quadratic variation, essentially carries over to higher dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-005: We also prove that this lower bound is tight, in the sense that it is the convex lower semicontinuous envelope of the specific relative entropy.
elements.sent-2411-006: This is a novel result even in dimension one.
elements.sent-2411-007: Finally we establish closed-form expressions for the specific relative entropy in simple multidimensional examples.
elements.sent-2411-008: Keywords: Martingales, relative entropy, specific relative entropy, Ganter's inequality.
elements.sent-2411-009: Mathematics Subject Classification (2010): Primary XXX; Secondary XXX.

elements.sent-2411-019: To wit, in \cite[Satz 1.3]{Ga91 Gantert observed that, if $X$ is a martingale and has an absolutely continuous quadratic variation under $\mathbb Q$ (say, $d\langle X \rangle_t/dt=\sigma^2_t$, $\mathbb Q$-a.s.), then \begin{equation}\label{eq:Gantert_h} \textstyle h_1(\mathbb{Q}|\mathbb{B}^1)\geq \mathbb{E}_{\mathbb Q}\left[\int_0^1 F_1(\sigma^2) dt\right ],\end{equation} where $\mathbb B^1$ denotes the standard one-dimensional Wiener measure and \begin{equation}F_1(x):=\frac{1}{2}\{x-1-\log(x)\}.\end{equation}.}

elements.sent-2411-266: Since, without loss of generality, $X$ is positive definite such that $X^{1/2 X^{1/2 =X$ the left hand side can be written as: \begin{equation}\det (X^{1/2}(\lambda I + (1-\lambda) X^{-1/2} YX^{-1/2})X^{1/2})=\det X \det (\lambda I+(1-\lambda ) Z)\end{equation} for $Z:= X^{-1/2 YX^{-1/2}$.}}}
elements.formula-2411-152: \begin{equation}\det (X^{1/2}(\lambda I + (1-\lambda) X^{-1/2} YX^{-1/2})X^{1/2})=\det X \det (\lambda I+(1-\lambda ) Z)\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-267: While the right hand side: \begin{equation}\det X^{1/2} (\det X^{-1/2}YX^{-1/2} )^{1-\lambda}\det X^{1/2}= \det X^{1/2} (\det Z)^{1-\lambda}\det X^{1/2} .\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-153: \begin{equation}\det X^{1/2} (\det X^{-1/2}YX^{-1/2} )^{1-\lambda}\det X^{1/2}= \det X^{1/2} (\det Z)^{1-\lambda}\det X^{1/2} .\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-268: So we need to show that \begin{equation}\det(\lambda I+(1-\lambda)Z)\geq (\det Z)^{1-\lambda}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-269: Further, we notice that $Z$ is clearly symmetric and also positive definite.
elements.sent-2411-270: Indeed, the smallest eigenvalue of $Z$ is given by \begin{equation}\begin{split} \textstyle \min _{|\mu|=1}\mu ^T(X^{-1/2}YX^{-1/2}) \mu \textstyle = \min _{|\mu|=1}(X^{-1/2} \mu)^TY(X^{-1/2} \mu) \textstyle =\min _{|\mu|=1} |X^{-1/2} \mu|^2\frac{(X^{-1/2} \mu)^T}{|X^{-1/2} \mu|}Y \frac{(X^{-1/2} \mu)}{|X^{-1/2} \mu|}. \end{split}\end{equation} Now considering the eigenvalues of $Z$, $(\gamma_i)_{i=1, \dots, l}$, it remains to show: \begin{equation}\textstyle \prod_{i=1}^l(\lambda +(1-\lambda)\gamma_i) \geq \prod_{i=1}^l \gamma_i^{1-\lambda},\end{equation} i.e.\ \begin{equation}\textstyle \sum_{i=1}^l \log (\lambda+(1-\lambda)\gamma_i) \geq (1-\lambda)\sum_{i=1}^l \log \gamma_i\end{equation} which holds by the concavity of the logarithm term by term.
elements.sent-2411-271: Now we observe that $F_l(X)\geq 0$ and $F_l(X)= 0$ iff $X=\mathcal I$.
elements.sent-2411-272: Indeed, if $(\sigma_i)_i$ are the eigenvalues of $X$, then we know that $F_l(X)=\sum_i F_1(\sigma_i^2)$.
elements.sent-2411-273: The result follows since clearly $F_1(\sigma^2)\geq 0$ and $F_1(\sigma^2) =0$ iff $\sigma^2=1$.
